items.q: Are AI and robots the same thing?
items.correct: No - AI is software that exhibits intelligent behaviour, while a robot is a physical machine; robots may or may not use AI, and most AI runs without any robot body.
items.misconception: Yes - anything with AI is a robot and every robot has AI.
items.random_wrong: Yes - AI is just the brand name for industrial robots.
items.why: Science fiction fuses them. Separating the physical machine from the decision-making software is exactly the distinction exam questions test.

items.q: Is a machine learning system given explicit rules for every situation?
items.correct: No - it is trained on data and learns the patterns itself; that is what distinguishes it from traditional rule-based programming.
items.misconception: Yes - programmers write a rule for every case the system might meet.
items.random_wrong: Yes - machine learning systems copy their answers from the internet.
items.why: Students extend their experience of IF-statements to all software. ML inverts the approach: examples in, rules inferred - which is also why training data quality matters.

items.q: Does automation remove the need for humans entirely?
items.correct: No - people are still needed to design, program, monitor and maintain automated systems and to handle unexpected situations.
items.misconception: Yes - an automated factory can run forever with no human input.
items.random_wrong: Yes - automated systems repair and reprogram themselves completely.
items.why: The empty factory floor misleads. The human work moves from doing the task to supervising the system - different jobs rather than no jobs.

items.q: An automated system is made up of:
items.misconception: Only a keyboard and a screen
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sensors, a microprocessor and actuators. “Only a keyboard and a screen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is a typical characteristic of a robot?
items.correct: It can be reprogrammed to carry out different physical tasks
items.misconception: It must look like a human
items.random_wrong: It cannot work for more than an hour
items.why: It's It can be reprogrammed to carry out different physical tasks. “It must look like a human” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Learning patterns from data and feedback
items.misconception: Being rebuilt with new hardware each time
items.random_wrong: Following a fixed list of rules that never changes
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Learning patterns from data and feedback. “Being rebuilt with new hardware each time” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: One advantage of automating a production task is:
items.correct: Consistent operation around the clock without tiredness
items.misconception: The system designs new products by itself
items.why: Correct answer: Consistent operation around the clock without tiredness. “The system designs new products by itself” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A robotic arm welds one joint every 30 seconds. How many joints can it weld in one hour?
items.why: The real figure is 120 joints. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
